retrieved_docs.title: Clinical Practice Issues in American Indians and Alaska Natives -- Epidemiology -- Life Expectancy and Overall Health
retrieved_docs.content: The 2015 life expectancies at birth for AIAN are 77.5 years, which very close to the national average of 79.8 years for NHW. The life expectancy for AIAN women is 80.3 years and 74.7 years for men. For NHW, the projected life expectancies are 82.0 years for women and 77.5 years for men. This is a marked improvement in the overall life expectancy compared to 1968, when President Johnson has stated that the average life expectancy of AIAN is 44 years compared to the national average of 65. However, differences within different states exist. For example, in Alaska, a state with a 15% AIAN population, the age-adjusted mortality rate is 1222.6 per 100,000 AIAN population, which is almost 3.5 times higher than NHW (393.7 per 100,000 NHW population). Likewise, in Montana, where 9.3% of the population identifies itself as AIAN, the age-adjusted mortality rate is 1386.9 per 100,000 AIAN population, which is almost double compared to NHW (678.1 per 100,000 NHW population)

retrieved_docs.title: Clinical Practice Issues in American Indians and Alaska Natives -- Epidemiology -- Life Expectancy and Overall Health
retrieved_docs.content: The 2018 Infant Mortality rate in the AIAN population continues to be double at 8.15 per 1000 live births compared to 4.63 per 1000 live births in the NHW population. In some states, the differences in IMR are more vivid- For example, in Alaska, where AIAN constitutes 15% of the population, the IMR is 14.5 per 1000 live births, compared to 4.8 per 1000 live births in NHW. Likewise, in Montana, where 9.3% of the population identifies itself as AIAN, the IMR is 12.5 per 1000 live births, compared to 5.9 per 1000 live births in NHW.

retrieved_docs.content: In 2018, 17.4% AIAN population reported being in fair or poor health compared to 10% of the NHW population.

retrieved_docs.title: Public Health Considerations Regarding Obesity -- Issues of Concern -- Public Health Impact of Obesity
retrieved_docs.content: Life expectancy: Obesity causes serious illness and decreases the average life expectancy. Adult obesity strongly predicts early death. The Framingham Heart Study, a prospective cohort study, revealed that adults who were obese at 40 years lost 6 to 7 years of their expected lifespan. However, in obese people who smoked, the years of life lost almost doubled. [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Determinants of Health -- Summary / Explanation
retrieved_docs.content: Introduction The Office of Disease Prevention and Health Promotion's (ODPHP) Healthy People 2030 vision is for "a society in which all people can achieve their full potential for health and well-being across the lifespan." To achieve this, 3 areas, "well-being, healthy life expectancy, and summary mortality and health," were identified to measure the health of the population (ODPHP. Healthy People 2030 ).

retrieved_docs.title: Kallmann Syndrome -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Kallmann syndrome alone is not associated with decreased life expectancy, but its possible association with heart conditions, osteoporosis, and reduced fertility may separately impact the patient's health and longevity.

retrieved_docs.title: Edwards Syndrome -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The live birth prevalence of Edwards syndrome ranges from 1 in 3600 to 1 in 10,000. In the past 2 decades, the prevalence of trisomy 18 has increased due to an increase in the average maternal age. The prevalence of Edwards syndrome varies by country and termination policies. In the United States, the overall prevalence of Edwards syndrome is approximately 1 in 2500, and the liveborn prevalence is 1 in 8600. [5] [6] The prevalence is higher in females compared to males, with a ratio of 3:2. However, fetal loss is higher in males compared to females, and females have better survival rates compared to males.

retrieved_docs.title: Osteoporosis in Females -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Worldwide, up to 37 million fragility fractures occur annually in patients older than age 55, which equates to 70 fractures per minute. [13] The European Union has designated fragility fracture as the fourth most burdensome noncommunicable disease (behind ischemic heart disease, dementia, and lung cancer). [14] Moreover, there is a large economic burden, which will continue to increase alongside the aging population. The economic burden in the United Kingdom is around £4 billion per annum and €56 billion in the European Union. In the United States, the Bone Health and Osteoporosis Foundation estimates a cost of approximately $19 billion annually. [15] [16] Apart from cost, fragility fractures are a significant health burden, with the European Union noting 1,180,000 quality-adjusted life-years lost, with twice as many in females compared to males. [17] Furthermore, 26,300 life-years were lost in the European Union due to incident fractures in the year 2010. [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Thoracic Aorta Aneurysm -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: In the United States, about 13000 patients die because of aortic disease each year, and TAA is the 18 most common causes of death among all individuals. [8] [13] TAA has an incidence of 10 cases per 100000 patient years and a prevalence of 0.16 to 0.34%. [9] The incidence of TAA is increasing due to better diagnostic imaging and increased life expectancy in the general population. [9] Patients with familial TAA have an average age of presentation at 56.8 years, while patients with TAA due to other causes present around 64.3 years. [9] More men develop TAA, while more women develop worse clinical outcomes and have an increased risk of dissection. [6] [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Progeria -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Werner syndrome is estimated to occur in 1 out of 100,000 live births worldwide. It has a high prevalence in Japan, where the frequency has been reported as high as 1 in 20,000 to 40,000 live births. The prevalence in the United States has been reported as 1 in 200,000 live births.

retrieved_docs.title: Female Infertility -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: In a study conducted by the National Survey of Family Growth that interviewed 12,000 women in the United States, the prevalence of infertility decreased with the increase in the woman’s age. [4] As a woman gets older, her chances of infertility increase. In women aged 15 to 34 years, infertility rates ranged from 7.3% to 9.1%. In women ages 35 to 39 years old, the infertility rates increased to 25%. Lastly, women from ages 40 to 44 years had a 30% chance of infertility. [4] Worldwide, infertility rates are higher in Eastern Europe, North Africa, and the Middle East. Worldwide, 2% of women aged 20 to 44 were never able to have a live birth, and 11% with a previous live birth were unable to have an additional birth. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Klinefelter Syndrome -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Fewer than 10% of cases are detected before puberty, and only 26% to 37% are identified overall, with most remaining undiagnosed. [11] [12] [13] The average age of diagnosis is 30 years, and the median lifespan is reduced by 5 or 6 years compared to the general male population. [14] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 1 -- Community 202
retrieved_docs.content: The medical framework links gestational age, birth outcomes, and perinatal disorders through a hierarchical classification system. A gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is identified as a precursor to a liveborn outcome, indicating that infants born after this duration can survive outside the womb. Duration of gestation is positioned as a broader category that inversely encompasses liveborn, underscoring that the length of pregnancy defines the viability of a live birth. Conditions associated with short gestation and low birth weight — collectively described as Short gestation; low birth weight; and fetal growth retardation — serve as a primary classifier for liveborn infants, reflecting the clinical importance of prematurity and insufficient fetal growth in determining neonatal status. This same condition is also classified as ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن} (“birth at the highest degree of prematurity, unspecified weight”), reinforcing its role as a marker of

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2320
retrieved_docs.content: The gestational period of 29‑30 weeks completed is directly linked to the outcome of a liveborn infant, indicating that reaching this stage of pregnancy can result in a newborn who is alive at birth. In the broader classification of pregnancy timelines, the concept of Duration of gestation encompasses liveborn as a specific category or outcome, reflecting that livebirth is considered one of the possible results within the overall spectrum of gestational durations. This relationship underscores the clinical relevance of gestational age in determining the likelihood of a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2825
retrieved_docs.content: INFANT PRETERM LOW BIRTH WEIGHT is clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature), indicating that infants born preterm with low birth weight often experience growth restriction that results in reduced adult height. In turn, قصر القامة is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), suggesting that short stature in this context reflects severe underlying health challenges that increase the risk of death shortly after birth. Recognizing the link between low‑birth‑weight preterm infants, subsequent short stature, and heightened neonatal mortality can guide clinicians in early monitoring and intervention strategies to improve outcomes for this vulnerable population.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 157
retrieved_docs.content: Overall, the model demonstrates that gestational age, birth weight, and fetal growth metrics intersect with diagnostic classifications, perinatal disease categories, and health‑service utilization, providing a comprehensive framework for understanding, classifying, and managing conditions that arise around the time of birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1449
retrieved_docs.content: Preterm low‑birth‑weight infants are clinically associated with قصر القامة (short stature). Short stature itself is clinically associated with وفاة حديث الولادة (neonatal death), indicating that children who remain short may have an increased risk of dying in the newborn period. Additionally, تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر (threatened preterm labor) is clinically associated with قصر القامة, suggesting that pregnancies complicated by early labor threats often result in offspring who develop short stature. Collectively, these relationships highlight short stature as a common clinical link among preterm low birth weight, threatened preterm labor, and adverse neonatal outcomes such as neonatal death, underscoring its relevance for risk assessment and early intervention in perinatal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 4 -- Community 2865
retrieved_docs.content: Duration of gestation refers to the length of time a fetus remains in the uterus before birth, and the classification of a birth as liveborn is encompassed within this gestational timeframe; therefore, liveborn status is defined by the specific duration of gestation at delivery.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1721
retrieved_docs.content: Infant preterm low birth weight, early or threatened labor, threatened preterm labor (تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر), short stature (قصر القامة), and neonatal death (وفاة حديث الولادة) are interrelated clinical concepts. Infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with short stature, indicating that babies born prematurely with low weight are more likely to develop reduced adult height. Short stature itself is clinically associated with neonatal death, suggesting that children who remain short have an increased risk of dying in the newborn period. Early or threatened labor is used to classify threatened preterm labor, and threatened preterm labor is also clinically associated with short stature. Together, these relationships highlight that low birth weight and threatened preterm labor can contribute to short stature, which in turn is linked to higher neonatal mortality, underscoring the importance of monitoring growth and labor complications in perinatal care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 4 -- Community 2885
retrieved_docs.content: Infant preterm low birth weight is clinically associated with short stature (قصر القامة), indicating that children born prematurely with low birth weight have an increased risk of developing reduced height later in life.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2821
retrieved_docs.content: Threat of premature labor (تهديد بالمخاض المبتسر) is clinically associated with short stature (قصر القامة), indicating that individuals who are shorter in height may have an increased risk of experiencing preterm labor. This association highlights the importance of recognizing short stature as a potential clinical indicator when assessing pregnant patients for the likelihood of premature delivery, thereby informing monitoring strategies and preventive care measures.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 102
retrieved_docs.content: and cardiovascular complications are a subset of the overall perinatal condition spectrum. Collectively, these interrelated entities illustrate how gestational age, birthweight, and fetal growth trajectories define a network of classifications—from specific gestational milestones (29‑30 weeks) to broad perinatal disorder groups—providing a comprehensive framework for understanding, diagnosing, and managing health issues in liveborn infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2763
retrieved_docs.content: Short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation constitute a severe perinatal condition that is classified as an extremely premature birth of the highest degree with unspecified weight (ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}). This classification highlights that the infant is born far before term, exhibits markedly reduced body weight, and shows evidence of impaired intra‑uterine growth, all of which place the newborn at high risk for morbidity and mortality and necessitate intensive neonatal monitoring and intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2849
retrieved_docs.content: Poor reproductive history and elective termination of pregnancy are clinically associated with preterm labor (مخاض قبل الأوان). This relationship indicates that women who have experienced a suboptimal reproductive history or who have undergone an elective termination of pregnancy may be at increased risk for developing preterm labor. Recognizing these associations can help clinicians identify patients who require closer monitoring and targeted interventions to prevent or manage preterm labor, thereby improving maternal and neonatal outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2432
retrieved_docs.content: Cervical shortening, unspecified trimester, is mapped to short cervical length in pregnancy, indicating that the condition of cervical shortening at any stage of gestation is understood as a short cervical length—a key obstetric measurement used to evaluate the risk of preterm birth and guide patient management.
